_note: Hand-authored executive summaries for each council member. Displayed on scorecard PDF above Performance Pillars. Update when a member's pattern changes materially. Each entry: archetype (short label) + summary (2-3 sentences, plain language, no acronyms).

OKeefe.summary: Shows up, stays on-topic, and asks about costs before voting. In a council that almost never votes no, O'Keefe is the closest thing to a member who consistently puts the taxpayer's question first. No grand gestures; just the work.

Blackaby.summary: Competent, present, and focused without being exceptional. Neither champions taxpayer interests aggressively nor undermines them — a reliable vote who stays inside the lines of core city business and avoids the ideological detours that consume colleagues' bandwidth.

Bartlett.summary: Asks pointed fiscal questions in chambers but votes yes alongside everyone else and missed five of seven major fiscal votes including both budget adoptions. Has a documented pattern of attaching himself as co-author to proposals after the primary work is done — gaining the visibility of authorship without the accountability of having shaped the item. The skepticism is real; the follow-through is not.

Kesarwani.summary: Focused, present, and effective in chambers — but willing to reach for new revenue when the political path points that way. Authored the sales tax survey referral that launched Berkeley's 2026 tax campaign infrastructure. Not a fiscal champion, not a fiscal vandal.

Taplin.summary: Explicitly believes Berkeley is 'underbonded' and has built the institutional scaffolding to match — authoring referrals for regular bond issuance and leading infrastructure bond campaigns. A genuine counterpoint: Taplin has been an effective champion for Automated License Plate Readers, navigating real political resistance to deliver a concrete public safety result. The exception doesn't change the governing disposition: new debt is his preferred tool.

Humbert.summary: The council's most verbose member (115 words per turn on average), with the highest investment in the existing homeless services apparatus. Shows up and engages substantively — the question is what the engagement is in service of. A Homeless Services Orthodoxy score of 62 puts him firmly in the spending-orthodoxy camp.

Ishii.summary: The Mayor who has structured constituent access to prevent organized accountability — groups are excluded from his office hours — and whose transcripts contain near-zero fiscal concern language. Agenda-setter for a council that has never adopted the foundational fiscal balance test the City Auditor recommends.

LunaParra.summary: Highest Homeless Services Orthodoxy score on the council, highest abstention rate, and a Taxpayer Alignment score near the floor. When difficult votes arrive, LunaParra tends to abstain — using the voting mechanism to avoid registering a position rather than to register one.

Tregub.summary: Third-highest transcript voter alignment score — talks about fiscal concerns constantly in chambers — but a Taxpayer Alignment composite of zero. Authors bond campaigns, lobbied for a county spending measure from his official constituent email list, and scheduled a community input meeting three weeks after voting to approve the project it was nominally about.
